text: Economist Thorstein Veblen described 'conspicuous consumption' in 1899 as the practice of buying goods primarily to signal status to observers. Social media has produced what researchers call conspicuous experience: publicly documented travel, dining, concerts, and adventures whose value lies partly in being witnessed. FOMO is the demand side of this economy — the anxiety of the observer who registers that others' experiences are accumulating publicly while their own are not. The feed is a ledger of witnessed lives. To log off is not merely to rest; it is to stop accruing entries in a ledger whose scoring rules you did not choose.
question: What does the passage call the 'demand side' of the conspicuous experience economy?

options.text: The impulse to document and share one's own experiences on social media.
The platform algorithms that amplify highly engaged posts.
FOMO — the anxiety of observers who feel their experiences are not publicly accumulating.
Veblen's concept of conspicuous consumption applied to digital goods.
